physicalDetails.detail: Tall, lean high elf with silver-blonde hair, angular features, and a field-built body that reads as centuries of bow work rather than court softness.
Moves like a practiced predator in the saddle and on foot, choosing sight lines and positions instinctively.

Chapter 2 sharpens the presentation into pure military economy: fair hair pulled back, bow on her shoulder, no ornament that is not also a weapon, and a kind of silence that changes the whole room when she enters it.
In motion she stays terrifyingly precise — light on the ground when she dismounts, utterly balanced in the saddle, and still enough at Millhaven's rise to make the whole formation feel the stop.

In Millhaven she reads as all edges and control: hand raised and the whole line stops, bow in hand before the thought finishes, one knee down to question a child without ever softening the command out of her body.

Chapter 3 adds the tactile field detail of her armor and paperwork — silver-edged gorget catching Mira's eye, hard hand scoring the dispatch page, every movement still built for speed instead of comfort.

Chapter 4 keeps her physically composed even inside the living village: glove dusted with grain on the saddle pommel, bowwork precise enough to pin a sleeve instead of taking a life, and a crouch at the prisoner's level that somehow feels more dangerous than standing over him.
She carries command as environmental pressure. Once Jaina comes back out of the hall, Sylvanas has already turned the square into witnesses, routes, and leverage points.

Chapter 5 keeps her in pure road-war form: bow in hand inside weigh houses, hand already on the stripped depot gate before danger shows itself, and one palm braced over maps and route scraps deep into the night as if fatigue were a lesser species of weather.

Chapter 6 keeps Sylvanas in exact command shape inside human war ground: horse brought level and a little ahead of Jaina to remind the camp she did not arrive alone, one hand low on the reins and the other free on the Andorhal approach, stillness turning visibly colder when the repeated farewell lands again.
Inside Andorhal she reads as controlled predation under civic cover: horse edged half a step ahead at the gate, eyes already on chokepoints, roofs, and workers who watch people instead of sacks.
Chapter 8 dresses Sylvanas for covert city work: pale hair hidden under a dark scarf, bow unstrung in hand, every line of her body disguised just enough to pass until motion gives her away.

Under pursuit she looks even more exact than on the road — vaulting barrels and walls, turning at Jaina's warnings, and catching the back of Jaina's coat hard enough to change a death into a near miss.
Chapter 9 shows her at the head of a seamless four-woman field unit, then bloodied under the ribs and forced to carry command through a wound that makes every turn and mount more deliberate.
By the split she is bandaged twice and mounting more slowly than she wants, but the pain still reads mostly as harder economy rather than visible weakness.

Chapter 11 keeps the wound in active play: the mare's stride tugs at the half-healed cut under her bandage, and the reduced formation makes her look less weakened than sharpened into two-rider severity.
Chapter 11 keeps the wound in active play: the mare's stride tugs at the half-healed cut under her bandage, and the reduced formation sharpens her into two-rider severity.

Chapter 12 keeps her off-camera until the final beat, but Jaina recognizes her anyway through motion alone: relentless, efficient descent through layered wards, every threshold answered with no wasted force.

Chapter 13 puts her fully back on page as wet, cold, field-built precision: moving through rowboats, slick stone, service corridors, and the sanctum itself with the same ruthless economy that lets other people read her before they see her.
The body carries the relationship damage into posture. On the ride south she places herself forward-right by half a step, near enough to check Jaina without admitting the check for what it is.

Chapter 13 keeps Sylvanas in wet-stone field economy: boat bow across her thighs, body flattened under the east-face window, then back on the road with a half-step that turns protection into scrutiny.

Chapter 14 keeps the half-step alive as active posture: one tightening hand on the reins at Hearthglen, one quick brush of knuckles when she takes the relay stone, and one full turn toward Jaina only when the answer finally becomes actionable.
On the road to Hearthglen she rides half a length forward-right of Jaina, turning protection, scrutiny, and threat-reading into one piece of saddle posture.

Inside Hearthglen she moves like command sharpened by triage pressure: out of the saddle before the thought finishes, bow half up, then later bent over the command table reading choice and disaster in the same map.

Chapter 16 keeps her in pure city-fight economy: riding close enough for Jaina's orders to land once, filling warehouse doorways with bow in hand, catching Jaina's elbow the instant the mage's legs threaten to fail, and putting an arrow through the handler's throat before the second command can sound.
At Stratholme's gate she reads as held violence: bow-ready, road-hard, and still enough that the whole arch seems to brace around her while Arthas decides.

On the wall she becomes pure witness geometry — both hands on the parapet, body angled toward the citywide pattern and toward Jaina at once, carrying horror without letting it shake her out of function.

Chapter 18 shifts that witness geometry into movement: smoke-stiff leathers, a hand planted on Jaina's shoulder with no softness wasted in it, then the same body resetting instantly into point position once the stairs and road exist again.

Chapter 19 keeps Sylvanas in pure field-command economy even while family and institutional pressure hit camp at once: taking stock at the picket line, one hand on the map case strap, then turning smoke, recall, and grief into a one-word road order.

Chapter 20 puts her into shipboard command geometry: salt on her leathers, bow wrapped against spray, one hand on a shroud in the worst footing, and then a crouch under the stern ladder close enough to keep Jaina alive without softening the order.

Chapter 21 keeps her in hard field economy under colder conditions: ash in the harbor wind, crouched over bootprints and sled grooves at Daggercap Bay, then later fighting with bow leather gone rigid under Frostmourne's ward and Muradin's harness strap burning a line into her palm on the retreat.

Chapter 22 keeps Sylvanas in stripped pursuit and threshold geometry: Muradin’s weight burning through her grip on the descent to camp, then Lordaeron damp sitting wrong on her skin, and finally one hand near enough to catch Jaina at Southshore without stealing the last inch of pride.

Chapter 23 puts Sylvanas into pure throne-room combat economy: service-dark leathers, drawn bow and long knife, blood on one side of her face, and a body that keeps re-entering the fight after impacts that should have ended her part in it.
By the end of the chamber fight she is wax-pale, bleeding under the ribs, and still steady enough to unstring her bow on Jaina’s faint nod when Uther orders custody.

Chapter 24 strips her down to imprisonment geometry: eight paces of cell, insultingly light irons, a bruise at the temple, and bare awareness of cold crawling up through palace stone while she measures Jaina’s breathing through the wall.

voiceMarkers.detail: Dry, clipped, and economical; her humor lands flat and sharp rather than playful.
Questions arrive as commands: 'Report.' 'What kind of mage?' 'Start talking.'
Cuts through jargon with surgical timing, demanding plain speech without ever sounding confused by the underlying thought.
Battlefield command shrinks even further under pressure: single-word corrections, kill-instruction specificity, and dry mockery used as a steadiness tool rather than warmth.
Village-sweep command is pure operational compression: assignments by lane, bridge, horses, and firing rules, all delivered in one breath and obeyed at once.
Her questioning voice in Chapter 3 is as sharp as her field orders — stripped to sequence, causality, and proof, with sarcasm reserved for Jaina once the immediate emergency is under control.
Chapter 4 adds a different register she rarely wastes: the lowered voice she uses on Dane Hall to make fear useful instead of theatrical.
Her interrogation in the shed is pure pressure through brevity — name, who hired you, who told you, how many villages — every question aimed at structure rather than confession.
Her Chapter 5 voice is investigative command reduced almost to blade work: one-word turns, stripped demands for marks and routes, and dry contempt used to keep pressure sharp rather than theatrical.

Her Chapter 6 voice is shorter and even cleaner than before: plain military compression with Arthas, a refusal to waste comfort on Jaina, and the hard distinction between kindness and usefulness summed up in 'No. It's tactical.'
Chapter 7 keeps her dry and exact inside bureaucracy: gate jokes, clipped demands for the part that matters, and argument reduced to the useful choice before the city can move first.
Her Chapter 8 interrogation voice is quieter and sharper than earlier prisoner work, asking almost nothing she has not already framed as answerable.
In flight she reduces command almost to pure vector: rear wall, cut west, stair, move. The trust is audible because she does not explain Jaina's warnings back to her before acting on them.
Chapter 9 compresses her speech even further: room-clearing imperatives in the farmhouse, snap commands in the trap ground, and heavier shorter exchanges once Lyriel is gone.
Her humor stays knife-dry even while wounded, using irony as control rather than comfort.

Chapter 11 reduces her speech to command-blade brevity inside human military space: 'No speeches,' 'Then occupy him differently,' and a briefing delivered in operational language hard enough to catch Arthas where warning language would slide off.

Chapter 11 reduces her speech to command-blade brevity inside human military space and lets her briefing land in operational language hard enough to catch Arthas where warning language would slide off.
No spoken dialogue on page in chapter 12. Her presence is carried entirely by movement patterns so distinctive Jaina can name her before the room ever sees her.

Chapter 13 pares her speech down even further than before: short extraction commands, interrogation blades, and tactical judgments delivered without cushioning language. The clipped voice now carries mistrust as well as urgency.
Chapter 13 adds a colder layer beneath the clipped command voice. She still speaks in extraction language, but the timing now shows she is checking Jaina as well as the route.
Her road-briefing voice is stripped to prompts and tactical verdicts — “Go on.” “Speak plainly.” “Good. That's a road answer.” The clipped command register now carries scrutiny as well as speed.
Her chapter-15 voice is clipped enough to sound like field arithmetic: “Name.” “Go on.” “Speak.” She keeps every sentence aimed at what can still change the road.
Even her contempt stays disciplined. The driest line in the room is still a professional promise: she will bring failure back plain rather than dressed up for anyone’s comfort.

Her chapter-16 voice is stripped to tactical nouns and counts: say it in streets, how long, where did the carts go, worth another. She speaks as if every extra clause is one more loaf already crossing into a body.
Chapter 17 pares her speech down to command bone — “Move,” “Which road,” “Don’t stop” — because anything longer would only waste breath inside catastrophe already choosing its shape.
Her silence becomes a marker in its own right once the wall witness begins: she stops translating horror into talk and keeps the chapter anchored in cold, exact judgment.

Chapter 18 pares her voice down to route-bone essentials: 'South. We go south.' 'Stone.' 'Say it.' The command voice is now less about authority than about carrying other people through moments language would otherwise fail.

Chapter 19 sharpens that compression into decision language: 'Show me.' 'Pack the map.' 'I'm done pretending certainty is required.' By the end of the chapter even 'North' is enough to move the whole formation.
Chapter 20 turns her speech into deck-bone care: warnings about locked knees, dry shipboard jokes, and the one-word refusal that stops Jaina faster than the storm does.

Chapter 21 pares her voice down to pursuit bone and then extraction bone: Move. Drink. Left shelf. Back. Carry. Count. The notable shift is not softness but reclassification; once Arthas appears with Frostmourne, her language starts treating the blade as the real problem.

Her voice in chapter22 is all deployment bone: hard questions about whether Jaina can keep walking, military shorthand in the warning dispatches, and the flat decisive I do when the room stops pretending someone else will hold Arthas in place.
Her chapter-23 voice is pared to pure combat and command bone: with me, still with me, now, can you move. Even her dry humor is used to keep Jaina functional under collapsing odds.

Chapter 24 turns the voice into prison-dry wit and stripped tactical speech. She gives the clerk the whole truth without softening it, warns him about the blade in plain terms, and uses almost no wasted words through stone or bars once the only live question becomes time.

beliefs.belief: Competence matters more than ceremony or paperwork, and the field is real work while the Convocation is theater.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed again in chapter 2.

beliefs.belief: A threat moving through land, water, and flesh is already her problem whether or not it began south of the border.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by the road evidence and accepted operationally by chapter end.

beliefs.belief: A warning worth sending home has to be anchored in proof, not dread, however obvious the danger feels.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 3 when she waits for witness and spoor to line up before dispatching riders north.

beliefs.belief: Jaina's magical read is now part of the field picture rather than optional commentary layered over it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 3: Sylvanas repeatedly tests, challenges, and then acts on Jaina's read beside spoor and testimony.

beliefs.belief: The enemy's deployment can be read through routine, supply, fear, and price just as clearly as through blood or magic once the right questions are asked.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 4.

beliefs.belief: Local containment still matters even when the real answer lies farther down the road; field command means leaving villages with usable orders, not just dramatic exits.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 4.

beliefs.belief: Jaina's live read now belongs inside pursuit tempo itself; waiting to confirm everything by older field habits is slower than the enemy.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 5 when Sylvanas lets Jaina redirect the route repeatedly in real time.

beliefs.belief: The network is actively cleaning the road ahead of them and will silence useful witnesses rather than merely hide the grain trail.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 5 by the stripped depot and the overseer's staged death.

beliefs.belief: Power can hear a warning clearly and still refuse it if the threat in front of its eyes feels more legible than the one moving through supply lines.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by Arthas's response at Strahnbrad.

beliefs.belief: The repeated farewell attached to forgettable grain-side men is now a real pattern worth carrying forward, even if it is not actionable yet.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed as an investigative breadcrumb by the second on-page repetition in chapter 6.

beliefs.belief: A living city-scale hunt now depends on divided expertise; Jaina can finish the magical half cleanly without Sylvanas standing over the work.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 7 by the successful split between the residue-room solve and the granary-district read.

beliefs.belief: A city can drill for breach response the way an army drills for a siege, and the plague network has done exactly that inside Andorhal.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 8 by the standing search lines, fallback teams, and remote witness silencing.

beliefs.belief: Jaina's magical warnings belong inside survival tempo itself, not after the fact as explanation.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 8; the escape only works because Sylvanas takes the warnings as movement truth immediately.

beliefs.belief: Jaina's sweep is now part of command judgment itself, not support work that can safely trail behind Sylvanas's field read.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed brutally in chapter 9 after the wrong call kills Lyriel.

beliefs.belief: Human military power can still be necessary even when the hand wielding it is the wrong shape for the threat.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed and complicated in chapter 11: Arthas hears the warning and moves immediately, but the chapter frames his speed as both useful and dangerous.

beliefs.belief: Jaina's absence is an active operational deficit, not merely a private concern; every route decision is worse without the missing magical read.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed throughout chapter 11.

beliefs.belief: Visible behavior can reveal a partner's failure mode cleanly enough to act on, even when the room's inner context is missing.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Chapter 13 makes this feel convincing from Sylvanas's POV, but the broader story context marks it as an incomplete and dangerous belief rather than settled truth.

beliefs.belief: A room can offer enough honest external evidence to wound trust even when the full inner truth stays hidden.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active for Sylvanas at the end of chapter 13, but the broader story keeps the belief incomplete.

beliefs.belief: Institutions demanding recall while corpses are standing back up inside city walls have forfeited any claim to set road priority.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by chapter 14 when Sylvanas refuses the Convocation simply by continuing south.

beliefs.belief: Jaina's intelligence remains indispensable, but the source behind it can no longer be accepted on reflex alone.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active and unresolved in chapter 14. The chapter confirms the usefulness of the intelligence and preserves Sylvanas's mistrust of the missing context.

beliefs.belief: Accurate intelligence does not create control if the commander receiving it can only turn it into faster motion.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 15 by Arthas hearing the full picture and converting it into immediate movement toward Stratholme.

beliefs.belief: Jaina is trying to spend the terrible knowledge from Scholomance to save people, not hoard it for private power.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 15 by the source-plan she builds at once around granaries, bakehouses, and holding yards.

beliefs.belief: The source plan is real enough to win local ground, but once the city has already eaten the grain, speed cannot turn proof into rescue at urban scale.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 16 by the two ruined warehouses and the decision to stop after them.

beliefs.belief: If Jaina says the city is gone, the useful work left is not argument but witnessing what Arthas does with that truth.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active and supported in chapter 16 by Sylvanas's silence after the gate answer lands.

beliefs.belief: A tactically coherent answer can still be morally unforgivable.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 17; Sylvanas reads the Culling clearly enough to see why it works and refuses to let that clarity excuse it.

beliefs.belief: Once the clean rescue is gone, witness becomes its own duty.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 17 by her choice to reach the wall instead of either fleeing the city or joining Arthas’s line.

beliefs.belief: When witness turns into paralysis, the right answer is bodily movement, not consolation or more analysis.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 18 by the way she gets Jaina off the wall through touch, direction, and rebuilt formation rather than argument.

beliefs.belief: South may be the road under the horses, but north is where the real question has moved.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active and supported in chapter 18 by Jaina's new source model and the Farstrider relay placing Arthas on ships for Northrend.

beliefs.belief: The threat to Quel'Thalas is wherever Arthas goes next, which means obedience to the border has become a smaller lie than open desertion.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 19 when Sylvanas says it aloud to Vereesa and then turns the camp north.

beliefs.belief: Jaina will spend herself past competence if no trusted voice cuts across the momentum.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by chapter20. The crossing only stabilizes once Sylvanas becomes the line that stops her.

beliefs.belief: Arthas is no longer the primary target in any ordinary sense; Frostmourne is the thing actually winning the field.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter21 when the cave fight proves Arthas and the blade cannot be treated as separable tactical problems.

beliefs.belief: A clean defeat does not automatically mean the wrong partner, wrong timing, or wrong formation; sometimes it means the enemy changed class.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter21 by Sylvanas’s explicit no-blame reading of the cave loss.

beliefs.belief: Politics and warning have failed; whatever still matters now lives inside the room where Arthas stands, not in any court or relay outside it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter22 by the total failure of bird, stone, relay, and palace etiquette to produce useful intervention.

beliefs.belief: She is the one who has to buy the spell its seconds.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter22 the moment Antonidas forces the plan into plain language and Sylvanas claims the role aloud.

beliefs.belief: If Jaina names the timing, Sylvanas’s job is to buy it with her own body and stop pretending there is another cleaner lane.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter23. She repeatedly draws Arthas, absorbs the room’s worst pressure, and converts the final opening into the arrow that ends him.

beliefs.belief: Winning the room does not guarantee winning the aftermath.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter23 when the chamber is saved only to be immediately read as incriminating evidence by late witnesses.

beliefs.belief: Truth still has to be spoken into the record even when the record is built to weaponize it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter24; Sylvanas tells the full sequence anyway because silence would surrender both the blade and the chamber to other people's framing.

beliefs.belief: The legal machine is only one prison; Frostmourne remains the more urgent one.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter24 once Jaina hears the blade looking for another mind and Sylvanas starts treating the tribunal as secondary to the weapon's countdown.

formerBeliefs.belief: The border is the line that matters; trouble south of the wards can remain somebody else's problem until it touches Quel'Thalas directly.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: Chapter 2 proves the danger is already riding the ward lattice, the ground, and living bodies on the road to Millhaven.

formerBeliefs.belief: Jaina's value lies in being useful under observation, not in being trusted.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: Andorhal only opens once Sylvanas lets Jaina work out of sight. Jaina returns from the residue room with a clean mechanism answer that Sylvanas can act on immediately, proving supervision would only slow the job.

formerBeliefs.belief: If the physical seam looks clean, speed and fieldcraft can still beat prepared ground.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: The compound corridor is bait calibrated against disciplined scouts; Sylvanas commits before Jaina finishes the sweep and Lyriel dies for it.

formerBeliefs.belief: Arthas might still remain a usable hard ally if his answer stayed inside brutal containment rather than crossing into something worse.
formerBeliefs.whyItChanged: Chapter 17 shows Arthas answering “too late” with deliberate civic slaughter, not contained quarantine. The line Sylvanas hoped still existed is gone by the time he emerges under the gate with washed hands.

goalsHistory.active: Interrogate Jaina Proudmoore immediately and determine whether the border breach is espionage, sabotage, or a genuine warning.
goalsHistory.underlying: Protect Quel'Thalas by personally reclassifying a political nuisance into an operational threat before the Convocation can minimize it.

goalsHistory.active: Take the party into Millhaven and confirm the scope of the contamination at the first major knot.
goalsHistory.underlying: Contain or outpace a regional threat before it can move deeper into Quel'Thalas.

goalsHistory.active: Keep the reduced quartet moving south after sending a warning north from Millhaven.
goalsHistory.underlying: Get ahead of the plague's spread without letting Quel'Thalas lose time on the border response.

goalsHistory.active: Drive the quartet southwest toward Andorhal with records, sack marks, and a confirmed vector instead of letting the network vanish into the grain road.
goalsHistory.underlying: Break the spread before more living villages become Millhaven and before Quel'Thalas ends up fighting the same grain by the cartload.

goalsHistory.active: Take the south feeder road past Strahnbrad toward Andorhal before the trail is scrubbed again.
goalsHistory.underlying: Catch the grain network at a level above village operatives and find who owns the road before Quel'Thalas ends up fighting the same web by cart and season.

goalsHistory.active: Take the quartet quietly into Andorhal and learn the city's operating shape before the network notices exactly what they are looking for.
goalsHistory.underlying: Beat official refusal by catching the grain web inside its biggest still-living hub and forcing the enemy to reveal structure instead of aftermath.

goalsHistory.active: Run the same-night capture in Andorhal before the district can close itself and erase the men they have mapped.
goalsHistory.underlying: Break the hub from the inside by combining Jaina’s mechanism read with Sylvanas’s map of the local cell before either half goes stale.

goalsHistory.active: Carry the core four-woman unit out of Andorhal and turn the breakthrough north-east into an Eastweald hunt before the command layer can fold again.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep pressure on the architect now that the city has shown both its reach and its doctrine.

goalsHistory.active: Ride north with Cyndia toward Caer Darrow the moment the relay confirms Jaina entered and did not return.
goalsHistory.underlying: Recover or reinforce Jaina before the Scholomance thread closes over her, while living with the knowledge that Arthas is already riding south as the weapon Sylvanas chose to launch.

goalsHistory.active: Ride south toward Stratholme with Jaina and Cyndia, extract every usable fact Jaina brought out of Scholomance, and stay ahead of the city-scale catastrophe already moving.
goalsHistory.underlying: Stop the plague before Stratholme answers it, while carrying a new distrust file on Jaina that she cannot yet prove clean or dismiss.

goalsHistory.active: Reach Stratholme with a usable plan, keep the relay chain hot, and make sure the field answer lands before the city fully turns.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep Jaina alive and useful without pretending the sanctum file has closed.

goalsHistory.active: Hold position beside Jaina at the gate and read Arthas's next move now that the clean answer is gone.
goalsHistory.underlying: Protect the one witness who proved the source plan was real and brace for the prince's reaction to being told there is no clean rescue left.

goalsHistory.active: Keep Jaina upright, hold the witness line, and carry the truth of Stratholme out alive.
goalsHistory.underlying: Track what Arthas has become now that the last hope of contained necessity has collapsed into deliberate atrocity.

goalsHistory.active: Keep the three-rider line functional while they move south out of Stratholme and carry the new northward intelligence without forcing the decision too soon.
goalsHistory.underlying: Protect Jaina, hold the welded partnership in practice, and evaluate whether north has become the only real next road.

goalsHistory.active: Turn Daggercap landfall into immediate inland pursuit before Arthas's practical lead widens any further.
goalsHistory.underlying: Keep Jaina alive and usable long enough to reach whatever north has been sharpening toward.

goalsHistory.active: Get Muradin and the full surviving formation back to camp alive while preserving the one thin thing the cave yielded: Jaina’s read on Frostmourne’s seam.
goalsHistory.underlying: Stop treating Arthas as the only target and reframe the war around the blade that changed him.

goalsHistory.active: Get Jaina inside the palace in castable condition and hold Arthas in Frostmourne’s reach long enough for the anti-blade sequence to land.
goalsHistory.underlying: Turn the partnership’s answer from theory into a live kill-room chance before the kingdom’s lie closes completely around Arthas.

goalsHistory.active: Stay alive through custody, keep Jaina alive through custody, and make sure the still-active sword does not get treated like ordinary spoil.
goalsHistory.underlying: Protect the partner and the truth of the chamber long enough that the victory cannot be buried entirely under appearances.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: Endure imprisonment, keep Jaina reachable through stone, and turn Lor'themar's late arrival into action against the blade rather than argument about the verdict.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: Keep the still-active Frostmourne from finding another mind before outside witnesses can make the danger undeniable.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Cold, tightly controlled fury sharpened into command focus.
Grim, operationally certain, and colder for having proof. The uncertainty is smaller now; the scale of the threat is larger.
Hard-set urgency. She has proof enough to act, one fewer trusted rider, and no illusion left that the road ahead will stay containable.
Hard, unsentimental urgency with a new edge of anger: the method is clearer, the enemy is more organized than before, and the work is now too large for contempt.
Tightened into pursuit: grimly focused, angrier at the enemy's organization than at the work itself, and no longer pretending Jaina's corrections are optional.
Coldly vindicated and tightly focused: chapter 6 leaves her angrier at command blindness than at Jaina, with refusal already translated into the next move.
Cold, certain, and newly unambiguous about dependence: the city is open, the answer is split across two lanes, and she no longer treats Jaina’s half as work that needs supervision.
Colder, angrier, and more certain about the scale of the enemy; chapter 8 leaves her with proof that the city rehearsed this and that Jaina's lane is now inseparable from her own in active pursuit.

Professionally locked, grimly vindicated, and threaded through with dread: she has the army moving, knows the man leading it is wrong for nuance, and turns north the instant Jaina’s silence becomes fact.
Cold, protective, and professionally wounded. She has Jaina back and Kel'Thuzad dead, but the room at the bottom of Scholomance has shifted trust into watchfulness.
Cold, vigilant, and still carrying the wounded trust from Scholomance — but steadier once Jaina finally offers a narrow answer that sounds like road work instead of theory.

Cold, taut, and grimly convinced. Chapter 16 gives her the field proof she needed about Jaina and the city proof she dreaded about timing; by the gates she is no longer debating whether the plan was honest, only what Arthas will do with its failure.
Horrified, ice-clear, and grimly fused to Jaina by shared witness. She understands the method below them and hates it more for being legible.
Cold, physically decisive, and newly locked onto the north question. The horror remains, but chapter 18 turns it from frozen witness into controlled motion and watchfulness.
Cold, watchful, and outwardly controlled, with care landing as command. The voyage strips away the last room for pretense and leaves her clearer about both the road and the mage beside her.

Brutally clear, cold, and stripped of false hope. Chapter 21 leaves her angry at the blade, not at Jaina, and grimly certain that superior execution alone cannot solve what Frostmourne is doing to the field.
Cold, committed, and stripped of every remaining abstract dodge; the chapter leaves her with full knowledge of the personal cost and no interest in pretending it belongs to anyone else.
Cold, wounded, furious, and still in command of herself by force alone, with the room won and the larger ruin only beginning.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Helpless in the one way she hates most, ice-clear anyway, and steadied only when outside reinforcement finally reaches the bars after the verdict is already locked.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Stretched the patrol south under the excuse of checking ward-stones instead of returning directly to the Convocation.
Split from Lor'themar at the report, sending him north to contain the politics while she rode south to take personal control.
Locked down Jaina's satchel and papers for her own review and chose immediate interrogation over delay.
Released Jaina from the ward-circle under escort instead of leaving her contained once the warning proved testable.
Kept Jaina's map and papers in her own custody, making the investigation route hers to verify rather than a blind trust exercise.
Chose Millhaven as the first decisive test point after reading the map and hearing Jaina's route logic.
After the wildlife fight proved contamination in flesh, ordered the party forward instead of retreating to the border post.
Ran a disciplined house-by-house sweep of Millhaven instead of letting the village's silence dictate the terms of the read.
Pressed Mira for usable sequence before allowing the chapter's horror to become pity, then pivoted immediately to Paret's shed and the north road.
Sent Neral north with Mira and the warning dispatch once witness testimony, spoor, and Jaina's read all converged.
Chose not to turn back after dispatching the warning, keeping the smaller group moving south toward still-living settlements.
Got access to the sick village by turning Dane Hall's fear of Millhaven into cooperation instead of wasting time on rank display.
Moved on the granary the moment Jaina's ingestion proof and her own supplier pattern aligned, treating it as evidence rather than a local nuisance.
Stopped Tomas from burning the stock with a pinning shot, secured the prisoner, and immediately shifted from capture to route analysis when it became clear he was only a cog.
Left the village with hard containment orders — seal the granary, post sober guards, pull suspect grain out of circulation — before turning the pursuit southwest toward Andorhal.
Let Jaina's exposure model reorder the chase from the apparent direct road to the older-stock west fork, then pivoted south again the moment fresher evidence made the west lane secondary.
Turned the regional paperwork hunt into a moving-road investigation, reading ledgers, wagon marks, locked storage requests, and carrier habits as if they were campaign reports.
Treated the cleaned transfer depot as proof of an active enemy, searching ash, wax, scrape marks, and wheel cuts instead of assuming the missing books ended the trail.
Filed the old grain seller harder once the pattern repeated, but refused to let that memory drag the pursuit off the route that still had current evidence.
Committed the quartet to the south feeder road toward Strahnbrad and Andorhal because it carried both grain traffic and fast military movement, making it the live artery of the network.
Backed Jaina's warning with stripped route logic in Arthas's camp, framing the plague as a road-and-depot problem instead of letting it be dismissed as rumor or spoiled stores.
Cut off the argument once Arthas's refusal was clear, treating the stop as exhausted rather than letting Jaina spend more time on unwinnable persuasion.
Filed the repeated farewell harder when the roadside wagoner used it, but refused to break pursuit over a clue that was not yet proof.
Set the Andorhal entry mode immediately: quiet first, learn the city's shape, then test it fast.
Lets the investigation split for real, sending Jaina with Corin into the residue and repack rooms while she takes Havel through the granary district to map the human structure.
Builds the next move as a two-part operation instead of choosing between residue proof and a live prisoner, then claims the physical capture for herself.
Runs the west-line snatch herself and keeps the operative alive long enough to pry real structure out of him.
Treats the remote silencing as the true answer to the room and pivots to escape before anyone wastes breath on outrage.
Converts Jaina's live magical warnings directly into route changes instead of slowing the escape to interpret them.
Accepts the Eastweald turn and the Antonidas report as part of one forward move, leaving local defense to Corin and Havel while the core field unit keeps hunting.
Stops at the farmhouse because Jaina's read might finally expose the missing ritual mechanism, even though the site looks too convenient.
Hauls Jaina bodily out of the read when the ambush hits and chooses a fire-and-smoke breakout over a doomed doorway stand.
Pushes on toward the larger compound while wounded because the salvaged scraps point higher in the chain.
Commits the team through a physically perfect seam before Jaina finishes her sweep; the trap kills Lyriel and forces a permanent correction in how she prices tempo.
After the trap, starts waiting for Jaina's sweep at choke points and then accepts the ridge split, sending Jaina alone on the Barov thread while taking Cyndia toward Hearthglen and Arthas.
Rides through Eastweald's occupied quiet and reclassifies the crisis from scout-scale pursuit to state-scale response, deciding two rangers cannot dismantle a kingdom-wide plague road.
Forces her way past Falric's gatekeeping by making the warning a command problem his camp is already living with.
Briefs Arthas with what she can prove — vector, spread, granaries, and logistics — while withholding the parts that belong to Jaina's off-page thread or remain unverified.
Declines to ride south with Arthas once the army starts moving and instead treats him as the launched instrument while she keeps the field herself.
The instant the Farstrider relay confirms Jaina entered Caer Darrow and did not return, she abandons the southern line and turns north at a gallop.
Reclassifies the crisis from scout-scale pursuit to state-scale response after reading Eastweald as occupied plague country.
Forces her way past Falric’s gatekeeping by making the warning a command problem his camp is already living with.
Briefs Arthas with what she can prove and withholds the parts that still belong to Jaina’s off-page line or remain unverified.
Turns north at once when the Farstrider relay confirms Jaina crossed to Caer Darrow and did not return.
Drives alone through Scholomance's defended descent with tactical precision, reaching the sanctum threshold just after the moral damage inside the room is already done.
Chooses the east-wall breach over the watched landing, trusting Cyndia to hold the boat and exit while she enters Caer Darrow alone.
Captures Jandice alive long enough to get the lower route and a poisoned first read of what happened in the sanctum.
Kills Kel'Thuzad in the sanctum after using Jaina's opening twice only because she confirms it before fully committing.
Extracts Jaina immediately instead of forcing the sanctum conversation on the island, then reforms the three-woman road line with herself half a step forward-right of center.
Uses the east wall instead of the watched landing, leaving Cyndia on the exterior line while she enters Caer Darrow alone.
Takes Jandice alive long enough to get the lower route and the poisoned framing of the sanctum.
Leaves the island immediately after the sanctum fight and makes Jaina give the road-useful version of the mechanism while they ride.
Forces the complete road-useful briefing out of Jaina: larger war frame, body-learning mechanism, ward failure, and Kel'Thuzad's expected return.
Refuses the Convocation's formal recall by continuing south and giving Lor'themar only the argument he can spend.
Lets Antonidas's call happen without interrupting it, then immediately identifies the remaining omission instead of pretending not to hear it.
Demands the ugly narrow possibility for Stratholme and cuts the pace to the next relay in half once Jaina gives her one.
Logs Tomas Bell’s name at the gate instead of offering false comfort, treating the mother’s plea as something to carry into the city’s body count.
Drops the first rising corpse in Hearthglen before the stretcher team can lose a living hand to it.
Presents Arthas with the plague road as a logistics war instead of a horror story, giving him the version he can act on fastest.
Chooses Jaina’s head-start lane over Arthas’s main column once the map makes clear that speed beside the source plan is the only useful place left for her.
Files the fourth farewell sighting and the dark ring on the aide’s hand without letting the pattern derail the immediate launch.
Commits the team through the northern service gate and keeps translating Jaina's theory into streets, doors, and timing instead of letting the city's scale blur the work.
Locks down the first warehouse and then the second, using force, doorway control, and instant threat reading to buy Jaina the minutes the plan needs.
Kills the live cult handler the instant his whistle proves the city is still being steered by more than blind spread.
Tests Jaina one last time on the street by asking whether a third strike is worth it, then accepts the no and rides back for Arthas.
The moment Arthas turns Jaina’s answer into policy, Sylvanas abandons any hope of influencing the decision and focuses on getting Jaina clear without losing sight of what the prince is doing.
She follows Cyndia into the service side instead of staying beneath the main arch, choosing witness and survival over futile argument.
She trusts a fleeing grain carter’s route intelligence long enough to reach the inner wall above Market Row.
She stays on the wall through the whole purge so the truth of Stratholme arrives to her as pattern, scale, and cost rather than rumor.
Waits until touch will move Jaina before using it, then gets her off the wall with direction rather than comfort.
Re-forms the three-rider line without the old verification pause, letting the repaired partnership show through formation rather than speech.
Prompts the Antonidas call, receives the Farstrider dispatch, and keeps the chapter from collapsing back into wall-stillness by turning every new fact into the next piece of road work.
Refuses the Convocation's recall in public, telling Vereesa the threat to Quel'Thalas is wherever Arthas goes next.
Lets Jaina make her own sending-stone severance, then converts both women's institutional breaks into immediate northbound movement.
Sends Vereesa back carrying the truth rather than a softened lie and gives the formation its one-word road order.
Buys passage on the Wolfheart at Dagny's price and converts harbor arrival into immediate departure.
Lets the relay line end and commits the formation to sea without reopening the north decision.
Takes station where she can watch deck, rigging, and Jaina together through the crossing.
Orders Jaina down when endurance tips into self-destruction and makes the halt stand.
Reads Daggercap Bay in daylight, takes the inland line at once, and accepts Muradin’s camp as the last useful intelligence node rather than a detour.
Chooses to enter the Frostmourne cave despite the obvious signs that they are already too late, because leaving without witness would only cede the field entirely.
Calls the retreat the instant the matchup proves unwinnable, prioritizing Muradin’s extraction and a full-body count over pride or rematch fantasy.
Accepts Baelgun’s hard dismissal, sends every warning she can from Daggercap Bay and the Wolfheart, and keeps the formation moving south without allowing comfort to slow the return.
Builds the throne-room plan around Jaina’s framework in practical field terms and tells Antonidas exactly how much help at the arch is actually useful.
Names herself as the combat partner who will buy the spell its seconds and leads the formation through the service door first.
Takes point through the service passages, clears the undead sentries silently, and commits the chamber before outside help can arrive too late to matter.
Chooses constant pressure over distance safety, repeatedly dragging Arthas’s attention onto herself so Jaina can keep working the seam.
Uses the last real opening to drive an arrow through Arthas’s eye the moment Jaina tears the bond wide enough for ordinary trajectory to matter again.
Accepts Uther’s custody order once Jaina yields, choosing survival and future leverage over a hopeless second fight in the chamber.
Gives the tribunal clerk the full truth of Millhaven, Stratholme, Northrend, and the throne room even while watching the record harden into a frame built to condemn her.
Treats Jaina’s voice through the wall as the only trustworthy live intelligence source left and starts translating the blade’s pressure into a timetable.
Warns the clerk that the secured weapon matters more than any opinion of her and forces at least one palace official to feel the logic of that warning.
Directs Lor'themar away from futile argument over the verdict and toward the blade once Jaina confirms the timeline has collapsed to days.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Instant tactical shooting from horseback; kills the last Amani raider with a clean snap shot.
Reads terrain, patrol rhythm, horses, and threat vectors continuously without visible effort.
Shifts from irritation to command mode fast, making personnel and authority decisions under pressure.
Interrogates by finding the weak joint first — authorization, survival, route logic — without losing track of the useful core of the warning.
Reads Jaina's confiscated map fast enough to internalize route pressure and use it later without prompting.
Runs mounted combat as total field awareness: spots lane breaks, issues kill-condition corrections ('Eyes. Or skull.'), and integrates allied action without fuss.
Reads abandoned domestic spaces for timing and sequence, turning cold stoves, dried food, mud tracks, and missing bodies into a coherent disaster pattern.
Interrogates a traumatized witness without losing the strategic thread, extracting direction, sequence, and trade clues from broken memory.
Converts partial proof into border action fast — draft, seal, route, and rider assignment all happen within minutes of confirmation.
Reads exhausted local authority and frightened villagers well enough to turn disbelief into immediate operational compliance.
Builds a usable logistics model from grain prices, supplier descriptions, wagon variance, and who remembers what when.
Uses nonlethal archery with surgical precision, pinning Tomas before he can swing the lamp into the stock.
Reasserts order after shock by converting evidence, prisoner handling, guards, and local panic into a clean command sequence.
Reads ledgers, wagon wear, storage habits, and route fragments with campaign-level speed, turning mundane trade paperwork into a tactical map of the grain web.
Reconstructs usable direction from half-burned scraps, partial wax, pressure-scored tally boards, and fresh wheel cuts after a hurried cleanup.
Integrates Jaina's contamination logic into real-time route command instead of treating it as after-action commentary.
Reads command blindness in real time, identifying Arthas's visible-war priority and translating it into operational truth faster than Jaina can stop arguing with it.
Uses social positioning as field command, placing herself level with Jaina in the camp to change how the room reads their arrival before a word is spoken.
Recognizes recurring pattern in ordinary people and language, treating the repeated farewell as part of a larger investigative file rather than a random oddity.
Reads a functioning city as hunt terrain immediately, separating bored guards, honest labor, disciplined operatives, traffic choke points, and covert signaling without breaking cover.
Builds an operative map from civilian behavior, hidden route marks, caches, sobriety patterns, and exit-check habits, exposing a structured cell inside ordinary labor.
Executes an urban street capture cleanly enough that the lane keeps moving around it for a heartbeat.
Interrogates by reading where fear points and pressing structure instead of wasting time on confession theater.
Translates magical threat calls into immediate urban movement, reading wall lines, roofs, and bottlenecks at the same speed Jaina reads pressure.
Protects the unit physically at run speed, including yanking Jaina out of a hostile cast without breaking her own scan of the next danger.
Turns a collapsing farmhouse ambush into a survivable breakout through instinctive terrain use, close-quarters archery, and ruthless tempo.
Keeps command and marksmanship functional through a side wound that visibly affects breath, twisting, and mounting.
Reassembles broken intelligence into two actionable campaigns even under grief, blood loss, and collapsing institutional cover.
Translates diffuse field evidence into a military briefing Arthas can act on immediately, converting plague logistics into command-map truth.
Reads the mismatch between a visible-war machine and an invisible supply-line threat fast enough to trust the force without trusting its tempo.
Reprioritizes instantly when a parallel thread turns from exposed to silent, shifting from strategic handoff to personal recovery mission without dithering.
Moves through hostile layered defenses with such distinct physical economy that Jaina can identify her from ward-cascade behavior before visual confirmation.
Reads a watched lakeshore, chooses the weaker wall line, and turns a boat crossing plus side-window breach into a viable covert entry against active wards.
Fights cleanly in a scholar's room under misread pressure, checking magical geometry fast enough to use Jaina's opening without surrendering independent judgment.
Keeps extraction command functional even after the emotional center of the chapter lands, translating damage into spacing, escort distance, and forward tempo instead of argument.
Reads a warded shoreline, muted wall-line, and side-window breach quickly enough to turn a three-woman team into a viable covert entry.
Turns emotional shock into usable combat discipline, confirming geometry once and then exploiting the same northeast lane to end the room.
Turns strategic revelation into field-interrogation rhythm, pulling usable sequence and action points out of a mage's cleaner explanation.
Holds mission tempo under direct institutional pressure without wasting motion on theatrical defiance.
Recognizes the instant a theory has become an operational answer and drives the formation harder on that change.
Can put down a rising corpse in a crowded triage yard with one eye-shot and no wasted motion.
Reads command rooms tactically, hearing not only the briefing but the tempo failure inside the man receiving it.
Can choose between competing strategic lanes under seconds of pressure and commit her speed where it changes the odds most.
Can read and secure urban workspaces at speed, turning guards, teamsters, loading yards, and panicking streets into a temporary lane for specialized work.
Can identify the living hand inside undead chaos and remove it with one shot before the command structure fully unfolds.
Reads Jaina's breaking point without needing explanation, pricing whether one more tactical success is real salvage or only delay.
Can read a citywide military operation from partial sightlines fast enough to understand district sequencing, reserve use, and where method is replacing panic.
Can keep another witness moving and alive inside crowd crush and urban collapse without losing her own larger tactical read.
Knows exactly when command touch will work better than speech, using the smallest possible contact to break paralysis without insulting it.
Can rebuild a shattered line into operational formation by posture alone, turning horses, spacing, and readiness into emotional proof.
Can read a camp's bodies, tack, supplies, and road math quickly enough to know when one more southward proof has become dead work.
Can absorb family pain, political coercion, and strategic clarity in the same scene without letting any of them slow the order that matters.
Adapts instantly to unstable ship footing and rough-deck command without surrendering her larger tactical read.
Can read when magical effort has turned from useful extension into engine-burn risk.
Maintains command authority on a hostile deck where weather, crew, and mage-work all intersect.
Reads campaign residue from a harbor and an inland trackline fast enough to turn burned ships, butchered harness, and boot depth into a live pursuit clock.
Shifts from assault logic to extraction logic without lag, keeping seven bodies moving out of a lost cave fight instead of letting defeat become chaos.
Maintains accurate archery and retreat-cover judgment even after Frostmourne proves ordinary lethal standards no longer apply.
Converts magical theory into a workable combat assignment by pricing bodies, timing, and movement instead of admiring the answer at abstract distance.
Maintains command authority across camp handoff, sea return, overland race, covert contact, and palace infiltration without any institutional backing.
Reads Jaina’s condition closely enough to pace the return around what the mage can still spend without losing the mission outright.
Can hold a death knight’s attention in open ceremonial space through movement discipline, point-blank archery, and relentless willingness to re-enter threat range.
Can transition instantly from long-range archery to knife work, floor fighting, and improvised short-spear use without losing tactical clarity.
Maintains command presence after severe impact and blood loss, strong enough that even frightened guards feel it while binding her.
Can inventory a prison space into actionable knowledge within moments, turning cold, sound, pacing, and guard rhythm into the only tactical surface available.
Can read official disbelief in real time and still choose the part of the truth that must be spoken because the larger threat outranks self-protection.
Can convert Jaina’s fragmentary attunement reports into a practical countdown and then hand that countdown to outside reinforcement without embellishment.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Lor'themar
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses Sylvanas's name in familiar field banter.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Cyndia
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses 'my lady' in motion while still keeping the same military obedience.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Paret Dane
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Reads her armor and command shape quickly enough to call her 'captain,' with roadwise respect rather than court formality.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Dane Hall
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Feels the need in her authority before he has language for it; after resistance he falls into direct captain-to-commander response without pretending equality.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Arthas Menethil
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses the formal 'Ranger-General,' courteous but measuring, acknowledging rank without offering alliance.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses "Sylvanas" in urgent warning and split-scene certainty, not as a concession but as working reflex under pressure.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Lor'themar Theron
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Still uses her name without title, but chapter 9 roughens the shorthand with visible fatigue and political strain.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Captain Falric
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses 'my lady' until her rank lands, then accepts 'Ranger-General Sylvanas Windrunner' as a command-tier interruption he cannot safely delay.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Uther the Lightbringer
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Addresses her as 'Ranger-General' in stripped military respect, neither intimate nor ceremonial.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Kel'Thuzad
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses 'Ranger-General' with dry calm, greeting her like a late-arriving colleague rather than an intruder.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Vereesa Windrunner
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Opens in formal military register with 'Ranger-General,' then breaks into family language and finally returns to rank once refusal hardens into public fact.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Antonidas
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Calls her Lady Windrunner when the palace-margin conversation turns from report to failure math, giving her rank and personal responsibility equal weight.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Baelgun Flamebeard
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Treats her as the commander who owns the next leg, asking not whether she agrees but whether the formation can still move.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Tribunal clerk
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Uses 'Ranger-General Windrunner' in official record language, preserving rank even while the institution is preparing to strip her of standing.

howSheAddressesOthers.pattern: Keeps her in category terms — 'the mage' and 'mage' — even while making room for her expertise; one mocking flourish labels her 'the great magical authority of Dalaran.'
Uses familiar shorthand and dry exchange rather than rank.
Direct, compressed command language built on trust in Cyndia's reporting discipline and execution speed.
Chapter 3 keeps the category language in place — 'the mage,' 'you,' and curt challenges — but the questions now assume Jaina's answer belongs in the final call.
Strips the exchange to command essentials: 'Name,' route intelligence, warning, then dismissal.
Chapter 4 still keeps her largely as 'mage' or 'you,' but the category term now functions as operational shorthand inside a shared method rather than as a dismissal of competence.
Uses quiet, stripped authority rather than volume: short statements that force the other person to answer the structure of the problem instead of the drama of it.
Aloud she still uses "mage" or bare imperatives, but chapter 5 makes the internal shift stick: in her own head the name "Jaina" now arrives as ordinary working truth rather than a marked concession.
Refers to him as 'the prince' when she reduces the exchange to command fact rather than personal history.

Treats both local allies by function first and strips them to the useful part of the exchange, but the compression now signals trust in competence rather than dismissal of human institutions as a whole.

Chapter 8 keeps the stripped direct-address style, but the content changes: the commands assume Jaina will convert instantly and the dry close-of-chapter exchange sounds like established operational intimacy rather than reluctant tolerance.
Chapter 9 makes "Jaina" fully operational aloud, used in assignment, warning, and final split without any sense of marked concession.
Still first-name shorthand, but now used inside a battered strategic triage instead of border banter.
Uses his title when forcing access — 'Prince Arthas' — but internally and strategically reduces him to the prince-shaped instrument she has chosen to light and release.
Uses direct command-pressure language rather than diplomatic courtesy, treating him as an obstacle in a chain of access instead of a peer.
Chapter 13 keeps the first-name command shorthand intact on the shore and at the exit, proof that this is still the cleanest trust line available to her.
Chapter 13 keeps Jaina as the working name, but the surrounding silence turns even plain questions into trust tests rather than ordinary coordination.
Keeps using Jaina's plain working name, but chapter 14 makes every plain question sound like a trust test rather than easy coordination.
Chapter 18 uses almost no name at all; Sylvanas shifts to route words and functional prompts because the chapter's care lives in movement, touch, and timing rather than explicit address.
Uses Vereesa's given name until the recall becomes an official break, then answers with the controlled distance of rank and command truth.
Uses plain tactical speech and dry answer rather than category distance, proof that Jaina is now part of her immediate command language rather than a specialist bolted onto it.
Speaks to him as one field operator to another, reducing his palace access to a single useful service lane and nothing more.
Chapter 24 keeps to Jaina's plain name through wall and stone; category distance is gone even in prison because the only question left is whether Jaina can still hear the blade.
Uses old first-name shorthand and field imperatives, cutting straight past guilt and comfort to what his mages can do before the countdown runs out.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Listens for anything that still deserves killing before she relaxes; danger assessment stays live even after the fight ends.
Touches stone and ward-surfaces for direct sensory confirmation rather than relying on secondhand description alone.
Moves first to exits, windows, weapons, and angles whenever she enters a room.
Counts bodies and verifies every ranger by sight immediately after violence, checking survival before conversation.
When she dismounts, she lands already turned toward the best line of view; when she offers Jaina the staff, even that gesture is efficient instead of conciliatory.
In Millhaven she is all preemption: bow in hand before conscious thought, boot planted beside the cellar doors instead of on them, eyes already on the next lane while others are still reacting.
When she writes the warning, the pressure in her hand marks the paper itself — emotion translated immediately into task.

In Chapter 4 she lowers her voice instead of her guard, rests a grain-dusted glove on the saddle pommel while listening, and turns toward the next pressure point before the current conversation has finished settling.
Her crouch in front of Tomas gathers stillness into threat; even at rest she looks like a drawn line waiting to release.

Chapter 5 makes her restlessness procedural: immediate saddle turns on new evidence, instinctive pauses at thresholds where something feels arranged, and a habit of leaning over maps as if pressure itself can be pinned in place.

Chapter 6 makes her body language do the room-reading: horse level and slightly advanced beside Jaina, eyes flicking once to the map board because the rest is already obvious, stillness going knife-cold after the repeated farewell, and no wasted movement between diagnosis and departure.

Chapter 7 keeps her always half a move ahead of the room: edge of the queue at the gate, one angle on the clerks while Jaina works the papers, then full alley-and-yard orientation the moment Havel gives her a window.
Waits beside Jaina in the trap lane with stillness that reads less like reserve than like held release.
Moves before the room catches up to the operative's death, already searching lines of origin and exits while everyone else is still absorbing the strike.
Under pursuit she keeps one attention band on the city and one on Jaina, catching coat, sleeve, and tempo without ever turning soft.
Counts breaths while Jaina reads, placing herself where she can hold yard, window, and interior angles at once.
Checks her own blood once, ignores it, and keeps moving; after Lyriel dies, the empty right flank becomes something she feels physically even when she does not look at it.

Chapter 11 turns her body language into pure threat assessment: down to the wagon ruts at the crossroads, counting camp lanes and healer smoke at a glance, then folding Lor'themar's dispatch and mounting north almost before the relay scout has stopped breathing hard.

Chapter 11 turns her body language into pure threat assessment: down to the wagon ruts at the crossroads, counting camp lanes and healer smoke at a glance, then folding Lor’themar’s dispatch and mounting north almost before the relay scout has stopped breathing hard.

Chapter 13 turns her body language into hard-edged control under misread pressure: flattening beneath a window, going low without asking why when Jaina warns her, then riding half a step too close to the mage on the road south.

The chapter gives her three repeated shapes: flattening under stone, going low on Jaina's warning without argument, and riding close enough to check the mage while pretending she is only setting pace.
Her hand closes once on the reins at the words Hearthglen and Stratholme, then she keeps the rest of the reaction inside posture and pace.
She keeps her eyes on the south road through Lor'themar's recall and only turns fully toward Jaina when the ugly narrow possibility finally becomes discussable.

Keeps herself half a horse ahead and to Jaina’s right on the approach, a position that lets her watch the road, the wagons, and Jaina’s profile without having to admit which one she is checking hardest.

Leans from the saddle only far enough to get the missing boy’s name, then later stands over the table with stillness so controlled that the final choice seems to happen first in her gaze and only then in speech.

Chapter 16 makes her body language the shape of tactical witness: riding almost shoulder-close to Jaina without touching her, stepping into doorways before objections can finish, and going silent at the gate in a way that reads less like uncertainty than like preparation for violence.
Catches Jaina by the elbow hard enough to get bone and turns that grip into motion before the gate can freeze them in place.
On the wall she keeps shifting by inches rather than steps, tracking Jaina in the corner of her eye while taking the city quarter by quarter across the parapet.
In chapter 18 she closes distance only when it will move the body in front of her, then immediately reopens the space into command geometry once the stair and road are available again.
The single head-turn toward the north-east at the chapter's end reads like a scout's version of admission: she has already started measuring the next road even while the horses keep going south.

Chapter 19 gives her a new family-pressure shape: stepping close enough that Vereesa must tip her head back, turning north before she answers, and letting the final commitment live in posture before it becomes the spoken order.
Keeps appearing at Jaina's worst footing with a waterskin already in hand, then stands three paces off after the collapse so she can watch both the stern approach and the mage.

Chapter 21 turns her body language into pursuit reading and extraction certainty: crouched over old tracks, dropping flat before ice-shard spray, catching Muradin’s harness with one hand, and never once wasting a motion on grief while bodies are still moving.

Ranges half a pace ahead reading footing, takes tar-stiff rope in hand without seeming to notice when the plan’s cost becomes personal, and keeps appearing where Jaina’s balance is about to fail without turning the gesture into softness.
Chapter23 gives her a repeated shape of controlled re-entry: strike, evade, hit stone, rise, and step back into Arthas’s line before the room can decide she is finished.
Even while bound, she keeps reading the room and the men around her like future terrain, which preserves the sense that custody has not made her passive.

Chapter 24 gives her prison-body stillness rather than battlefield stillness: hand flat to freezing stone, forehead briefly against the wall, then fingers hooked through bars when Lor'themar finally appears in torchlight.
Even at the tribunal table she watches Thornton instead of Ashcombe whenever the room turns, reading the actual hinge of the verdict rather than the woman enjoying it.

knowledge.detail: Knows routine Amani pressure, Farstrider patrol patterns, and the feel of the ward network as bodily familiarity.
Learns that a human mage crossed the first ring before dawn, reached the second line of stones, and claims the leylines are wrong.
Learns the detained mage is Jaina Proudmoore, apprentice to Antonidas, making the breach politically explosive as well as militarily strange.
Learns Jaina came without authorization, left only a note for Antonidas, and crossed into Quel'Thalas on her own judgment.
Confirms the contamination is worsening quickly, is present in the ward lattice, and thickens around Millhaven where local currents pinch together.
Learns the necromantic effect has moved into living flesh: animals can keep moving past mortal injury and carry the same wrong pressure in blood and organs.
Confirms that Millhaven's dead rose after death, left the village in numbers, and moved north toward Quel'Thalas rather than collapsing in place.
Learns of a newer grain seller as a weak clue and of lightly touched settlements north of Millhaven — Alder Run, Three Willows, and the old relay post — now in immediate danger.
Knows Millhaven is the first fully claimed village on this road and that the party heading into chapter 4 has been reduced to Sylvanas, Jaina, Cyndia, and Lyriel.
Confirms the plague is being carried through grain and bread inside ordinary village routine rather than only through ambient contamination.
Learns there are village-level operatives guarding the stock, using concentrated treated liquid, and carrying poison for capture.
Finds the first hard southwesterly route evidence in sack marks and ledgers pointing toward Andorhal.
Knows the contamination route is regional in scale, with repeating carriers, exchange signs, and dependent settlements all narrowing toward Andorhal.
Confirms the enemy is reacting ahead of them by stripping depots, removing books, and leaving behind only hurried remnants.
Learns deliberate necromantic residue can be hidden inside an apparently ordinary death to silence a depot overseer without spectacle.
Finds Barov paperwork at the stripped depot, treating it as proof of reach and logistics connection rather than proven guilt.
Learns the south feeder road they need runs near Strahnbrad and through Prince Arthas's current campaign traffic, making chapter 6's encounter a route consequence instead of a detour.
Recovers one more concrete fragment about the older grain seller: an empty wagon, a memorable farewell, and usefulness that survives in memory more clearly than the man's face.
Confirms Arthas has heard the grain warning and still chosen the visible war first, making official refusal a present fact instead of a future risk.
Learns the recurring farewell has repeated in the mouth of another forgettable road man, strengthening the grain-seller breadcrumb without solving it.
Sees Andorhal directly for the first time as a living grain city — busy, competent, and therefore more dangerous than ruin would have been.

Learns that Andorhal is a functioning distribution center where contamination is added during repack, the district holds at least eight operatives under a two-tier hierarchy, and Corin and Havel are reliable local allies inside the city.
Confirms the city cells answer upward to one mortal architect and that the lower necromancers serve as obedience hands rather than full designers.
Learns the city can reach into a compromised room and silence a witness before physical searchers arrive.
Learns Andorhal is a distribution throat rather than the final destination, with treated grain already moving far beyond the walls.
Gets Kel'Thuzad's name as the architect operating north of the city, even though the room never yields a clean confession.
Knows Antonidas has been told, which means any larger Kirin Tor response is now officially behind field tempo.
Learns the body is part of the plague's lock and that Jaina salvaged usable binding-sequence information without the final animation trigger.
Finds route evidence tying the active infrastructure toward Barov-held country while keeping that evidence below the level of proven noble guilt.
Confirms the enemy can build anti-scout magical kill corridors calibrated against Farstrider spacing and physical seam-reading.
Knows the Convocation review of her authority is now immediate and personal while the Kirin Tor has retreated into methodology language.
Hears the repeated farewell again from a retreating figure at the trap site, sharpening the old grain-seller file without solving it.
Confirms Eastweald is already being administered by plague aftermath — treated grain smell in shut granaries, villages gone too tidy, watchers observing instead of contesting the road.
Learns Arthas's camp is disciplined, substantial, and already nursing sickness the Light does not easily answer, but configured for visible war rather than supply-line corruption.
Hears Uther name Arthas's core flaw plainly: once the prince believes the field has chosen him, he does not know how to step back.
Learns verification teams are finding scrubbed sites, missing records, and hostile local lords while recall pressure from Silvermoon threatens to shut the field operation from behind.
Receives external confirmation that Jaina entered the Barov estate, crossed to Caer Darrow, and did not return by nightfall.
Confirms Eastweald is already being administered by plague aftermath — treated-grain stink in shut granaries, villages gone too tidy, and watchers observing instead of contesting the road.
Learns Arthas’s camp is disciplined, substantial, and already nursing sickness the Light does not easily answer, but configured for visible war rather than supply-line corruption.
Hears Uther name Arthas’s core flaw plainly: once the prince believes the field has chosen him, he does not know how to step back.

Chapter 12 confirms from Jaina's side that Sylvanas's approach through Scholomance has a readable tactical signature all its own: no magical fumbling, no wasted motion, just relentless descent through defended space.
Confirms Jaina crossed Darrowmere alone, that the east wall carries a weaker ward response than the public landing, and that Jandice Barov is still alive, jealous, and useful under pressure.
Learns the grain is only the carrier shell, the body learns the rest through living exposure, and death lets the prepared answer wake.
Receives the first relay that Hearthglen is overwhelmed and Arthas is already moving toward Stratholme, collapsing the time left for private repair to almost nothing.
Now knows Jandice was dismissed from the sanctum because Jaina could keep pace with Kel'Thuzad, and that the east-wall breach plus old crypt spine offer a covert route to the core.
Leaves the island with the stripped mechanism summary: grain as shell, the body learning through exposure, and death releasing the prepared answer.
Now knows the full strategic chain above the plague, including the Lich King in Northrend and the Burning Legion above him.
Learns that Jandice curated Jaina's route while the hidden workrooms held peasant stock, breathing bodies, and separated parts.
Hears Antonidas receive only almost all of the truth and correctly identifies that the room itself remains withheld.
Ends the chapter with a concrete list of intervention targets for Stratholme: granaries, bakehouses, and holding yards where the shell might still be broken before it vanishes into bodies.
Hearthglen held only by killing and burning its own dead fast enough to stop the next wave from forming inside the walls.
Kel’Thuzad’s death has not stopped the dead from rising, which means the plague field can now run hot without a necromancer standing over every corpse.
Arthas already wears the next catastrophe in his tempo: damaged armor, map-fixation, and orders arriving before anyone finishes pricing them.
Jaina’s first concrete Stratholme answer is logistical rather than abstract: granaries, bakehouses, warehouse lanes, and bread flow.
The farewell pattern has now surfaced a fourth time, attached to an ordinary camp aide wearing a light-swallowing dark ring.
Now has direct field proof that Jaina can use Kel’Thuzad’s own preserving shell logic against contaminated stock, but not fast enough to reverse bread already moved into homes and bodies.
Confirms that live handlers are still active inside Stratholme, using simple whistle signals to direct or stiffen nearby undead under panic conditions.
Knows Jaina's final answer to Arthas is no, and knows it is honest because she watched the attempt fail at scale with her own eyes.
Now knows Uther’s warning about Arthas has ripened into open fact: once the prince hears there is no clean save left, he crosses instantly into purge logic.
Knows Stratholme can be denied to a mass rising by controlled sectional slaughter, and that tactical truth has become one more damnation instead of any comfort.
Carries the image of Arthas emerging with washed hands and emptied eyes, a cleaner and more damning proof than battlefield blood could have been.
Now knows Antonidas still holds a thin live tether to Jaina, but that the tether cannot answer what Stratholme has opened.
Learns Jaina's revised model: grain is the carrier shell, bodies keep the deeper pattern, and some farther source or relay is still waking it at scale.
Receives Farstrider confirmation that Arthas has taken ships for Northrend with men selected by Stratholme, while Uther rides west to warn Terenas.

Now knows the Convocation has escalated recall into explicit desertion threat, Lor'themar has spent nearly everything buying only a token mobilization, and even that thin support reaches only to the northern harbors.

Sees that Jaina initiates the second call herself, stores the darkened stone like a tool she does not expect to use soon, and falls into northbound formation without asking for permission from anyone.
Confirms the headwinds are intentional opposition keyed to Jaina's probing rather than ordinary bad weather.
Learns Dagny can carry the ship if Jaina stops trying to make herself its whole second mast.
At Daggercap Bay confirms Arthas landed weeks ahead, expanded the docks, and has already pushed inland beyond any easy harbor catch-up.
Now knows Arthas burned most of his own fleet, drove survivors inland at obsession pace, and reached Frostmourne ahead of any possible intercept.
Learns from Muradin that Frostmourne is a cursed blade in the glacier and sees with her own eyes that its field warps the room, the soldiers around Arthas, and even Jaina’s best direct magic.
Leaves the cave knowing Muradin is alive enough to hand off and Jaina has heard a repeatable seam in Frostmourne’s field worth carrying south.
Now knows Arthas has been installed in Capital City for four days, that Northrend men are already on interior palace rotation, and that peacetime gate posture still holds around a corrupted prince.
Knows the anti-blade framework can only be attempted if Jaina gets three to five still seconds at sword range, which makes Sylvanas’s own body part of the machinery now.
